questions.stem: Neurons are cells that carry:
questions.explanation: Neurons are nervous-system cells that conduct electrical impulses along their nerve fibres.

questions.stem: A long single nerve fibre projecting from a neuron's cell body is the:
questions.explanation: The axon is the long single fibre; dendrites are the multiple shorter fibres.

questions.stem: The multiple shorter fibres of a neuron are called:
questions.explanation: Dendrites are the multiple shorter fibres of a neuron; the axon is the single long fibre.

questions.stem: The resting potential of a neuron is maintained by pumping which ions?
questions.explanation: Sodium and potassium ions are pumped in opposite directions to establish and maintain the resting potential.

questions.stem: The energy for pumping sodium and potassium ions comes from:
questions.explanation: ATP powers the pumping of sodium and potassium ions to establish the resting potential.

questions.stem: The resting potential of a neuron is:
questions.explanation: The resting potential is negative inside relative to outside, due to ion pumping and membrane permeability.

questions.stem: A nerve impulse travelling along a fibre is an:
questions.explanation: Nerve impulses are action potentials propagated along nerve fibres.

questions.stem: A nerve impulse is described as electrical because it involves:
questions.explanation: The impulse is electrical because it involves the movement of positively charged ions across the membrane.

questions.stem: Which fibre conducts impulses fastest?
questions.explanation: Myelinated fibres conduct impulses faster than non-myelinated fibres; larger diameter also increases speed.

questions.scenario: Conduction speed is compared with axon diameter.
questions.stem: The correlation between them is:
questions.explanation: Conduction speed is positively correlated with axon diameter (and negatively correlated with animal size).

questions.scenario: A student calculates R² for conduction speed against axon diameter.
questions.stem: R² (the coefficient of determination) indicates:

options.text: How much variation in one variable is explained by the other

questions.explanation: R² measures the degree to which variation in the independent variable explains variation in the dependent variable.

questions.stem: A synapse is a junction between neurons and between neurons and:
questions.explanation: Synapses are junctions between neurons and between neurons and effector cells (such as muscle).

questions.stem: At a typical (chemical) synapse, a signal can pass:
questions.explanation: A signal passes in only one direction across a typical chemical synapse.

questions.stem: Which ion is taken up, triggering neurotransmitter release?
questions.explanation: Depolarization causes uptake of calcium, which acts as a signal for neurotransmitter release.

questions.scenario: A neurotransmitter diffuses across the synaptic cleft.
questions.stem: It then binds to:

options.text: Transmembrane receptors on the postsynaptic membrane

questions.explanation: Neurotransmitters diffuse across the cleft and bind transmembrane receptors, generating a postsynaptic potential.

questions.stem: Which neurotransmitter is used as the example, and is found at neuromuscular junctions?
questions.explanation: Acetylcholine is the example neurotransmitter and is found in many synapses, including neuromuscular junctions.

questions.stem: The synapses referred to in this topic are:
questions.explanation: The syllabus limits discussion to chemical synapses, which can simply be called synapses.

questions.stem: The cell body of a neuron contains:
questions.explanation: The cell body of a neuron contains cytoplasm and a nucleus, with nerve fibres projecting from it.

questions.scenario: The giant axons of squid conduct impulses quickly.
questions.stem: This is mainly because of their:
questions.explanation: Squid giant axons conduct fast due to their large diameter; myelination also speeds conduction in vertebrates.

questions.stem: Membrane polarization refers to:

options.text: A difference in charge across the membrane
The membrane having no charge difference

questions.explanation: Polarization is a difference in charge (membrane potential) across the membrane, negative inside at rest.

questions.scenario: Inside the presynaptic neuron, calcium acts as a:
questions.stem: Calcium's role here is as a:
questions.explanation: Calcium acts as a signalling chemical inside the neuron, triggering neurotransmitter release after depolarization.

questions.scenario: Neurotransmitter binds receptors and depolarizes the postsynaptic membrane.
questions.stem: This generates an:
questions.explanation: Neurotransmitter binding that depolarizes the postsynaptic membrane generates an excitatory postsynaptic potential.

questions.stem: Why does a signal pass in only one direction at a synapse?

options.text: Only the presynaptic side releases neurotransmitter and only the postsynaptic side has receptors

questions.explanation: Neurotransmitter is released only from the presynaptic membrane and detected only by postsynaptic receptors, giving one-way transmission.

questions.stem: An action potential is best described as:

options.text: A rapid, propagated change in membrane potential

questions.explanation: An action potential is a rapid change in membrane potential that is propagated along the nerve fibre.

questions.scenario: Conduction speed is compared with animal size.
questions.stem: The correlation is described as:
questions.explanation: Conduction speed is negatively correlated with animal size but positively correlated with axon diameter.

questions.stem: Nerve fibres of neurons vary in:

options.text: The element they are made of

questions.explanation: Nerve fibres projecting from the cell body vary in length; the axon is long and dendrites are shorter.

questions.stem: Neurotransmitters are released from the presynaptic membrane by:

options.text: Exocytosis of vesicles after calcium entry

questions.explanation: Calcium entry after depolarization triggers vesicles to release neurotransmitter from the presynaptic membrane.

questions.stem: Sodium and potassium ions are pumped across the neuron membrane in:
questions.explanation: The pump moves sodium and potassium in opposite directions across the membrane to build the resting potential.

questions.stem: The synaptic cleft is the:

options.text: Gap between presynaptic and postsynaptic membranes

questions.explanation: The synaptic cleft is the gap across which neurotransmitters diffuse from the presynaptic to the postsynaptic membrane.

questions.stem: Nerve impulses are propagated along:
questions.explanation: Action potentials are propagated along nerve fibres such as axons.

questions.scenario: During an action potential, sodium channels open.
questions.stem: This causes:
questions.explanation: Opening of voltage-gated sodium channels lets sodium in, causing depolarization.

questions.stem: This leads to:
questions.explanation: Opening of voltage-gated potassium channels lets potassium out, repolarizing the membrane.

questions.stem: Voltage-gated sodium channels open only when the membrane reaches the:
questions.explanation: A threshold potential must be reached for voltage-gated sodium channels to open, starting an action potential.

questions.scenario: An action potential propagates along an axon.
questions.stem: This propagation is caused by:

options.text: Local currents of sodium ions bringing the next region to threshold

questions.explanation: Diffusion of sodium ions (local currents) inside and outside the axon brings the adjacent region to threshold, propagating the impulse.

questions.scenario: An oscilloscope trace shows the membrane potential over time.
questions.stem: The number of impulses per second can be found by:

options.text: Counting action potentials in the trace over time

questions.explanation: Oscilloscope traces show resting and action potentials; impulse frequency is found by counting action potentials per second.

questions.scenario: In myelinated fibres, action potentials jump between nodes of Ranvier.
questions.stem: This is called:
questions.explanation: Saltatory conduction, in which the action potential jumps between nodes of Ranvier, speeds impulses in myelinated fibres.

questions.stem: In myelinated fibres, ion pumps and channels are clustered at the:
questions.explanation: Ion pumps and channels cluster at the nodes of Ranvier, so the action potential is propagated node to node.

questions.stem: They act by:
questions.explanation: Neonicotinoids are an example of an exogenous chemical (pesticide) that blocks synaptic transmission.

questions.stem: It acts by:
questions.explanation: Cocaine is an example of a drug that blocks reuptake of the neurotransmitter, prolonging its effect.

questions.scenario: An inhibitory neurotransmitter binds to the postsynaptic membrane.
questions.stem: The postsynaptic membrane becomes:
questions.explanation: Inhibitory neurotransmitters hyperpolarize the postsynaptic membrane, generating an inhibitory postsynaptic potential.

questions.scenario: Several presynaptic neurons feed into one postsynaptic neuron.
questions.stem: The combining of their excitatory and inhibitory effects is called:
questions.explanation: Summation combines excitatory and inhibitory inputs, determining whether the postsynaptic neuron reaches threshold.

questions.stem: The consequence of summation reaching threshold in the postsynaptic neuron is described as:

options.text: All-or-nothing (an action potential fires or does not)

questions.explanation: Postsynaptic depolarization has all-or-nothing consequences: an action potential fires only if threshold is reached.

questions.scenario: Free nerve endings in the skin detect high temperature or chilli's capsaicin.
questions.stem: These stimuli cause channels to open, letting in:
questions.explanation: Pain receptors open channels for positive ions in response to stimuli, reaching threshold and sending impulses to the brain.

questions.stem: Pain is ultimately perceived:

options.text: In the brain, after impulses travel there

questions.explanation: Pain receptors send impulses to the brain, where pain is perceived.

questions.stem: Consciousness is described as an emergent property because it:

options.text: Arises from the interaction of many individual neurons

questions.explanation: Consciousness emerges from the interaction of individual neurons in the brain, an example of an emergent property.

questions.stem: Which sequence correctly describes an action potential?
questions.explanation: Once threshold is reached, sodium influx causes depolarization, then potassium efflux causes repolarization.

questions.stem: Why does saltatory conduction speed up impulses?

options.text: The impulse jumps between nodes rather than moving continuously
It slows the impulse at each node

questions.explanation: In myelinated fibres the action potential jumps from node to node, greatly increasing conduction speed.

questions.stem: An inhibitory postsynaptic potential makes the postsynaptic neuron:

options.text: Less likely to reach threshold and fire
Unable to have a resting potential

questions.explanation: Hyperpolarization from an inhibitory neurotransmitter makes the neuron less likely to reach threshold.

questions.stem: Whether a postsynaptic neuron fires depends on the balance of:
questions.explanation: Summation of excitatory and inhibitory inputs determines whether the postsynaptic neuron reaches threshold and fires.

questions.scenario: An exogenous chemical increases the effect of a neurotransmitter by preventing its removal.
questions.stem: This is most similar to the action of:
questions.explanation: Blocking reuptake prolongs a neurotransmitter's effect, as cocaine does; neonicotinoids instead block transmission.
